hook: You already know how to count forward: 1, 2, 3, 4… and you can probably picture those numbers lined up in a row. But what happens when you owe someone $5, or the temperature drops below zero? Those situations produce numbers less than zero — numbers you may not have placed on a line before. How do we show both positive and negative numbers in one organized picture, and how do we use that picture to compare them?
concept: A number line is a straight line where every point corresponds to exactly one real number, and the numbers increase from left to right. Formally, the number line extends infinitely in both directions: negative numbers ($-1, -2, -3, \ldots$) sit to the left of zero, positive numbers ($1, 2, 3, \ldots$) sit to the right, and zero ($0$) sits in the middle as the origin. Equal spacing between tick marks means equal difference in value.
anatomy: Every number line has four key parts. The origin is the point labeled $0$, which separates positive from negative territory. Tick marks are evenly spaced points representing specific values; the gap between consecutive tick marks defines the scale (often $1$, but sometimes $2$, $5$, $10$, etc.). Arrows on both ends signal that the line continues forever in each direction — there is no largest or smallest number. Finally, each plotted point is a filled dot placed exactly at the location of a specific number, and it can carry a label (like $-3$ or $+4$) to identify it.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Plot the integers } -3,\; 0,\; 2 \text{ on a number line with scale } 1.
worked_example.description_latex: Before placing any dots, we set up the number line by drawing a horizontal line, marking the origin at $0$, and placing tick marks one unit apart in both directions. Getting the scale right first means every number will land in exactly the correct spot.

worked_example.expression_latex: 0 \text{ is at the origin (center tick mark)}
worked_example.description_latex: We always place $0$ first because it is our anchor — every other number is defined by how far it sits from zero. Mark a dot directly on the origin and label it $0$.

worked_example.expression_latex: +2 \text{ is } 2 \text{ units to the right of } 0
worked_example.description_latex: Positive numbers live to the right of zero, so we count two tick marks rightward from the origin and place a dot there, labeling it $2$. Moving right always means increasing value.

worked_example.expression_latex: -3 \text{ is } 3 \text{ units to the left of } 0
worked_example.description_latex: Negative numbers live to the left of zero, so we count three tick marks leftward from the origin and place a dot there, labeling it $-3$. Notice that $-3$ is farther from the origin than $-1$ would be, which is why more negative means 'further left.'

Because the number line increases from left to right, any number farther left is smaller. Reading our three dots from left to right gives us $-3$, then $0$, then $2$, which we can write as the inequality $-3 < 0 < 2$ — a statement directly supported by the picture we drew.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Plot } -5 \text{ and } 3 \text{ on a number line, then write an inequality comparing them.}
partial_example.description_latex: Start by drawing a number line with scale $1$, marking the origin at $0$, and extending tick marks at least to $-6$ on the left and $4$ on the right. The origin and scale are already set for you here.

partial_example.expression_latex: 3 \text{ is } 3 \text{ units to the right of } 0
partial_example.description_latex: Count three tick marks to the right of the origin and place a dot at $3$. This step is done for you so you can see the process for a positive number.

Now it is your turn: starting from the origin, which direction do you move to place $-5$, and how many units? Place the dot and label it. Remember, the negative sign tells you which side of zero to stand on.
Look at your completed number line. Which dot is farther to the left? Use that observation to write a correct inequality comparing $-5$ and $3$ using the $<$ or $>$ symbol.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Which number is represented by a point } 4 \text{ units to the left of zero on a number line?}
\text{Plot } -2,\; 1,\; -4 \text{ on a number line and list them in order from least to greatest.}
\text{Use a number line to determine which is greater: } -6 \text{ or } -1.
\text{Point } A \text{ is at } -3 \text{ and Point } B \text{ is at } 5 \text{ on a number line. How many units apart are } A \text{ and } B?

\text{At 6 a.m. the temperature was } -7^\circ\text{F}. \text{ By noon it rose to } 4^\circ\text{F}. \text{ Mark both temperatures on a number line and determine how many degrees the temperature increased.}

common_mistakes: Students often place a negative number like $-4$ only one or two tick marks from zero because they focus on the digit $4$ without moving the full four units left; the fix is to always count out loud, one tick at a time, while moving in the correct direction.

When comparing two negative numbers such as $-7$ and $-2$, students frequently say $-7$ is greater because $7 > 2$ in the positive world; remember that on a number line farther left always means smaller, so $-7 < -2$ no matter how large the digit looks.

Students sometimes mix up the direction of the inequality symbol after reading a number line, writing $3 > -5$ as $3 < -5$ because they confuse 'reading left to right' with 'the left number is larger'; a reliable check is to point your inequality symbol like an arrow toward the smaller (leftmost) number.
untested_variants: Number lines with a scale other than 1 (e.g., each tick mark represents 5 or 0.5), requiring students to identify or plot non-integer or scaled values.
Finding the coordinate of a point that is described as the midpoint or halfway between two given integers on the number line.
Using the number line to model addition or subtraction of integers by showing directed jumps (e.g., start at $-2$, jump $+5$, land at $3$).
Number lines displayed vertically (like a thermometer), where up is positive and down is negative, requiring students to reorient their left-right intuition.
